title: Bold and italic — but not for looks
why.no-emphasis: There is not a single emphasis. There are words in this text that weigh more — "Never" is a warning. Say so with <strong>.
why.used-b: You used <b>. It also comes out bold — but it means nothing. It only means "bold". <strong> means "this is important", and a program reading the page aloud will say it in a firmer voice. <b> it will read like everything else.
why.used-i: You used <i>. It tilts the letters, but it does not say why. <em> means "I am stressing this". The difference is not pedantic — it is the difference between saying something and merely looking like it.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: Sometimes one word in a sentence weighs more than the rest. A warning. A key word. Something that must not be missed.
blocks.3.text: <strong> comes out bold. <em> comes out italic. So far — obvious.
blocks.4.text: But that is not what they are for
blocks.5.text: Their names are not "bold" and "italic". <strong> means "this is strong, important". <em> comes from "emphasis" — a stress, a note of insistence.
blocks.6.text: The difference is not pedantic. A program reading the page aloud for a blind person will read <strong> in a firmer voice. If you put it there just because you liked the look, you lied to it.
blocks.7.text: Want something merely bold, without it being important? That is CSS's job. HTML says why, not how.
blocks.8.text: Put emphasis where it makes sense in your text.
